title: The Miranda Hypothesis: How Hamilton Poisoned Persona Evals - Jacob E. Thomas, Results Gen
summary: Jacob E. Thomas argues that persona-eval benchmarks fail to detect "Miranda distortion," where AI systems speaking as historical figures reproduce cultural composites from training data rather than primary documents. He proposes an epistemic simulation framework evaluated by domain experts using anachronism detection, documentary consistency, and contextual plausibility rather than fluency metrics.
writeup: Jacob E. Thomas argues that persona-eval benchmarks miss what he calls Miranda distortion: when AI systems role-play historical figures, they reproduce cultural composites drawn from training data (the Hamilton example points to popular portrayals) rather than primary documents. The talk matters because fluency-based scoring rewards convincing output without checking historical fidelity. The proposed alternative is an epistemic simulation framework judged by domain experts on anachronism detection, documentary consistency, and contextual plausibility instead of fluency metrics.
